scenario: Forge Bank runs 60000 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8 across 8 clusters of process applications. A platform engineer is sizing capacity for a new global broadcast that triggers all running loan-origination processes, and the team needs to know how a signal broadcast scales with the partition count and matching subscription count of the cluster.
question: What is the cardinality and partition behavior of a broadcast signal in Camunda 8.8?

options.text: The broadcast triggers all matching subscriptions and is sent to every partition, with network traffic scaling linearly with the partition count of the cluster.
The broadcast triggers only the first matching subscription per partition and is sent to a single owner partition selected by a hash of the signal name.
The broadcast triggers all matching subscriptions on the local partition only, and remote partitions receive a deferred replay through the export stream.
The broadcast triggers a single matching subscription cluster-wide and is sent to one partition selected by least-loaded routing on the gateway service.

a.text: Correct. A broadcast iterates over all matching subscriptions and is sent to every partition; the documented performance caution notes that this network traffic scales linearly with the number of partitions in the cluster.

b.text: Incorrect. Signal broadcast does not select a single owner partition by hash and does not stop at the first matching subscription; all matching subscriptions on all partitions are triggered.

c.text: Incorrect. Broadcasts are sent to all partitions immediately, not just the local one with a replay; the documented traffic pattern is per-partition fan-out at broadcast time.

d.text: Incorrect. A broadcast does not pick a single subscription cluster-wide; the documented behaviour fans out to all matching subscriptions across all partitions of the cluster.

explanation: A broadcast signal triggers every matching subscription and is sent to every partition. Both factors affect performance: many subscriptions can spawn many process executions, and partition fan-out makes network traffic scale linearly with the partition count.
